original_abstract: Modern high-performance object detectors increasingly rely on detection proposals to direct their search, effectively bypassing the computational burden of exhaustive sliding-window techniques. While these proposal methods have become a cornerstone of the field, the specific trade-offs they introduce remain poorly understood. In this study, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of twelve prominent proposal methods and four baselines, examining their repeatability and recall performance across the PASCAL, ImageNet, and MS COCO datasets. We further investigate how these methods influence the end-to-end detection accuracy of frameworks such as DPM, R-CNN, and Fast R-CNN. Our analysis reveals that for effective object detection, enhancing localization precision is just as vital as maximizing raw recall. To capture this relationship, we propose a new metric, Average Recall (AR), which rewards both high coverage and precise spatial alignment. We demonstrate that AR correlates exceptionally well with final detection performance. Our results highlight the systemic strengths and weaknesses of current approaches, providing researchers with the necessary insights and metrics to better select and calibrate proposal methods.
humanized_abstract: The increasing popularity of detection proposals in high-performance object detection algorithms means that the previous CPU-intensive full sliding window search is no longer needed. As these proposal methods are used in many detection frameworks, their trade-offs are not well-studied. We systematically evaluate twelve different proposal methods, along with four sliding window baselines, for repeatability and recall on the popular PASCAL, ImageNet and MS COCO datasets. In addition, we analyze the impact of proposal methods on state-of-the-art object detection algorithms such as DPM, R-CNN and Fast R-CNN. Our study shows that there is a strong trade-off between accurate localization and high recall. To quantify the relationship between the two, we introduce Average Recall (AR), which measures the ratio of true positive instances that have high overlap with the ground-truth, and has strong correlation with the detection performance of different algorithms. Our study sheds light on the strengths and limitations of the various proposal methods, and helps in choosing optimal proposal methods and parameters for high performance object detection algorithms.
